claim: The current motto uses Aptos Display at weight 690/820 with outcome tracking of -0.07em; the interaction creates the compressed poster feeling visible in the baseline.
application: Defines the control and identifies variables that must be separated.
limitation: The aesthetic diagnosis remains expert judgment rather than participant data.
confidence: High for implementation; medium for perception

claim: Inter is designed for computer screens, has a tall x-height, supports variable weights and is distributed under OFL 1.1.
application: Supports a precise 680 weight and readable mixed-case hero copy with self-hosting.
limitation: A screen-oriented font is not automatically the best brand font; the official source also notes that display-size design involves different trade-offs.

claim: Source Sans 3 was designed for UI environments and is distributed under OFL 1.1.
application: Provides a warmer humanist control against Inter.
limitation: UI suitability does not prove the best semantic fit for Final Prime.

claim: IBM Plex is a broad, open font family under OFL, but it is explicitly IBM intellectual property and IBM's own design language typeface.
application: Provides a technical, engineered comparison and exposes borrowed-brand risk.
limitation: Association strength differs by viewer and market.
confidence: High for provenance; medium for perceived association

claim: Typeface cues can alter brand perceptions and memory; congruence among typography, visual cues and copy improved memory in the tested advertising context.
application: Justifies judging the font in the whole hero rather than in an alphabet specimen.
limitation: Advertising stimuli and consumer-memory tasks do not directly predict homepage preference or conversion.

claim: Chunking and shorter lines can help reading, but high line-length variability can outweigh those benefits.
application: Supports phrase-preserving breaks without manufacturing a ragged six-line staircase.
limitation: Long-form reading tasks differ from a two-sentence hero motto.

claim: Line breaks can induce implicit prosodic boundaries, and matching those boundaries to sentence structure can reduce processing load in the tested sentences.
application: Supports breaking only at the two sentence or clause boundaries.
limitation: The experiment used English temporal questions, not marketing headlines.

claim: Text must survive 200% resizing and content must reflow at 320 CSS px without loss; large text needs at least 3:1 contrast.
application: Hard gate for the responsive typography and color choices.
limitation: Conformance does not establish aesthetic quality.

claim: Against #090b0d, the current white, cyan and fuchsia measure 18.31:1, 13.05:1 and 5.59:1 respectively.
application: Shows that the accent decision is about hierarchy and intensity, not minimum large-text contrast.
limitation: The calculation does not model glare, low brightness, display calibration or individual perception.

claim: At 320 px, every finalist has zero horizontal overflow. P4/P5/P6 use 131 px of motto height versus 165.1 px for P1/P2/P3; at 1440 px the corresponding heights are about 117 px versus 225.5 px.
application: Quantifies how the two-sentence family reduces the second-poster effect and advances the orientation content on narrow screens.
limitation: Geometry does not measure preference or comprehension.

claim: The first two-line prototype used nowrap and overflowed internally at 200% text size; after removing nowrap, all shortlisted structures have zero internal and document-level horizontal overflow at 200%.
application: Records a found-and-fixed failure instead of treating a clean screenshot as proof.
limitation: The research preview is not the production implementation; the same gate must be rerun after integration.
